title: Proceedings of GT2006

Proceedings of GT2006
ASME Turbo Expo 2006: Power for Land, Sea and Air
May 8-11, 2006, Barcelona, Spain
GT2006-91004
DESIGN OF A LEAN PREMIXED PREVAPORIZED CAN COMBUSTOR
M.R.J. Charest, J.E.D. Gauthier, and X. Huang
Carleton University
Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering
Carleton University
Ottawa, Ontario K1S 5B6
Canada

title: Proceedings of GT2006
text:
ABSTRACT
Increasingly, more stringent emissions regulations have ne-
cessitated new gas turbine combustor designs with low pollutant
emissions. Radically different modern designs have been devel-
oped to meet these requirements while maintaining high com-
bustion efﬁciencies and good ﬂame stability. While several pub-
lished methodologies for conventional combustor design exist,
none exist for modern ones.
This paper describes the development of a new preliminary
design algorithm for a modern lean premixed prevaporized (LPP)
combustor. It also introduces a new LPP combustor concept. The
approach used is multi-disciplinary in nature, applying empiri-
cal and semi-empirical models in the algorithm to capture com-
plex processes such as droplet evaporation, chemical reaction, jet
mixing, and heat transfer. The resulting set of procedures allows
a designer to quickly deﬁne the detailed geometry of the com-
bustor and provides an assessment of its performance.
The preliminary design procedures were veriﬁed using the
advanced numerical techniques of computational ﬂuid dynam-
ics (CFD). Reasonable agreement between predictions from the
preliminary design and numerical analysis was achieved which
indicated that the design procedures have been developed suc-
cessfully.

text:

ics (CFD). Reasonable agreement between predictions from the
preliminary design and numerical analysis was achieved which
indicated that the design procedures have been developed suc-
cessfully.

title: Proceedings of GT2006
text:
INTRODUCTION
Governmental regulations for gas turbine emissions are be-
coming increasingly more stringent. The demand for new im-
proved designs with ultra-low pollutant emissions is rapidly
moving to the forefront of combustor development.
Radical
modern combustor designs have emerged to achieve emission
requirements while maintaining the high combustion efﬁciency
and good ﬂame stability characteristics of conventional combus-
tors. Lean premixed (LP) for gaseous and lean premixed prevap-
orized (LPP) for liquid fuelled engines are two modern designs
proven successful at meeting governmental regulations.
Design of these new modern LP and LPP combustors for
gas turbines is complicated by several factors. First, detailed in-
formation about current designs and the tools developed for their
design are regarded as proprietary by large engine manufacturers.
Additionally, there exists no published detailed methodology on
their design since they are relatively new technology and each
design tends to be drastically different.
Several authors have published preliminary designs for con-
ventional aircraft gas turbine combustors [1–3]. However, they
do not apply well to modern premixed ones. Lefebvre [4] and
Mellor [5] have compiled extensive lists of empirical, semi-
empirical, and analytical tools. While speciﬁcally addressing the
design of conventional aircraft combustors, the authors also pro-
vide information and references for new modern combustors.
Using a multidisciplinary approach, a methodology for the
preliminary design of a LPP combustor was formulated. These
procedures are the initial step in the overall design process which
precede complex numerical analysis using computational ﬂuid
dynamics (CFD) and ﬁnite element analysis (FEA). The devel-
oped methodology provides a detailed set of geometry and an
estimate of its performance.
This paper describes the basic elements of the preliminary
design procedure and summarizes the results of its veriﬁcation.
A detailed description including its application and veriﬁcation
is presented elsewhere [6].
APPROACH
Figure 1 illustrates the LPP concept, which uses a can con-
ﬁguration. In this particular implementation, air discharged from
the compressor enters the combustor at Station 3.0.
The air
passes through a faired prediffuser before it is dumped into the
annulus. In the region just downstream of the sudden expansion,
the cross-sectional ﬂow area in the annulus is large to ensure that
any asymmetry in the ﬂow is avoided.
As the air accelerates through the annulus, a portion is ad-
mitted by a row of holes in the liner to dilute the hot combustion
gases. The other portion of the air ﬂows through the annulus
where it cools the outside of the liner wall. One characteristic of
modern premixed combustion is that less air is available for liner
wall cooling. Therefore, wall cooling is enhanced using two dif-
ferent devices: trip strips on the cold side of the liner wall and a
thermal barrier coating (TBC) on the hot side.
The annulus air used for combustion is dumped into a
plenum that supplies the premixer. Inside the premixer, this air
passes through two concentric, counter-swirling axial swirlers.
Liquid fuel is injected into the resultant highly sheared ﬂow as
a ﬁne droplet spray where it evaporates and forms fuel vapour.
This vapour mixes with the surrounding air to produce a com-
bustible mixture.
A third axial swirler is used to induce a strong swirl compo-
nent to the fresh fuel/air mixture as it enters the liner for combus-
tion. This ensures the formation of a strong recirculation vortex
inside the liner and promotes stable combustion.
Operating Conditions.
The combustor must operate
stably and efﬁciently with low pollutant emissions over a wide
range of operating conditions. These operating conditions are
known prior to performing the combustor design through ther-
modynamic engine calculations or experimental testing.
For simplicity, the static temperature and pressure every-
where inside the combustor are assumed equal to the stagnation
values. This is a reasonable assumption since the Mach num-
ber everywhere is below 0.3. Thus, the following conditions are

temperature T3, the outlet temperature T4, and the inlet pressure
P3. Additionally, the density of the air at the inlet is given by the
ideal gas law:

The overall equivalence ratio,

(∆T = T4 −T3) is estimated by assuming equilibrium combus-
tion at constant pressure. While the combustion process will not
generally achieve equilibrium in practice, the assumption is valid
since the heat release, and consequentially the formation of CO,
occurs fast when compared to other reactions such as the forma-
tion of NOx.
The equilibrium constant approach, discussed in detail by
Turns [7], is used to compute φoverall. The overall equivalence
ratio is related to the operating conditions and fuel ﬂow as fol-
lows:

(2)
Airﬂow Distribution.
The airﬂow distribution is se-
lected to provide the desired stoichiometry in the primary and
dilution zones, which are illustrated in Fig. 2. As a result, the
fractions of the total air ﬂow in the various combustor passages,
Wi, are resolved.
Premixed combustors operating below 1900 K were deter-
mined to exhibit little NOx formation if the fuel and air was uni-
formly mixed [8]. A lower ﬂame temperature of 1800 K is se-
lected to minimize the peak value in any local hot spots caused by
unmixedness, which is present in all LPP combustors. Inherent
issues with combustor stability and excessive CO are expected if
operating any closer to the weak extinction limit.
The primary zone equivalence ratio, φPZ, is computed us-
ing the procedures discussed in the previous section. It is set to
provide the desired ﬂame temperature of Tfl = 1800 K. Further-
more, the dilution zone equivalence ratio is equal to the overall

Combustor Sizing.
Combustor sizing refers to the def-
inition of the reference (casing) diameter, Dre f , to provide suf-
ﬁcient stability without incurring excessive pressure losses. The
reference diameter, illustrated in Fig. 2, deﬁnes the total ideal
ﬂow area inside the combustor. All operating points (i.e., idle,
full power) are considered and the smallest size that provides
stability over the entire range of operation is chosen.
Speciﬁcation of the size for a premixed combustor is difﬁ-
cult as design data is not currently available. Thus, the premixed
combustor is scaled from an equivalent conventional combus-
tor. The scaling parameter chosen for this algorithm is the lean
blowout velocity, which is related to ﬂame stability.
The casing size for most industrial combustors is dictated by
the overall pressure loss, ∆P3−4. For conventional combustors,
the cross-sectional area of the casing is determined from [9]:
Are f =

Suitable values for ∆P3−4/P3 and ∆P3−4/qre f are chosen by the

The liner diameter must be chosen carefully. It would appear
desirable to select the largest diameter possible and reduce the
velocity of the ﬂow within the liner. This increases the residence

title: Proceedings of GT2006
text:
INTRODUCTION

coming increasingly more stringent. The demand for new im-
proved designs with ultra-low pollutant emissions is rapidly
moving to the forefront of combustor development.

title: Proceedings of GT2006
text:
time in the combustor and promotes stability. However, increas-
ing the liner diameter for a ﬁxed casing diameter results in a re-
duction of the annulus ﬂow area Aan. The smaller area results in
higher annulus velocities that decreases the static pressure in the
annulus. Therefore, a larger liner diameter is undesirable since a
high static pressure drop across the liner admission holes is nec-
essary to provide adequate penetration of the jets. Sawyer [1]
suggests that AL/Aref should be kept within 0.60-0.72 for con-
ventional combustors.
The combustor is resized for premixed combustion to main-
tain the stability of an equivalent conventional combustor [10].
The scaling parameter used here to measure stability is the lean
blowout velocity. For a bluff-body ﬂame holder, the lean blowout
velocity for homogeneous fuel-air mixtures is deﬁned as fol-
lows [11]:

title: Proceedings of GT2006
text:
Figure 4.
DIFFUSER GEOMETRY.
sure recovery coefﬁcient.
The static pressure recovery coefﬁcient is a measure of the
diffuser effectiveness at converting kinetic energy into static
pressure. It is estimated based on the diffuser dimensions us-
ing the widely accepted performance chart for conical diffusers
created by Sovran and Klomp [13].
The pressure losses across the sudden expansion correspond
well with the theoretical dumping losses [2]. Applying the mo-
mentum equation assuming uniform, steady, incompressible, and
frictionless ﬂow:
∆Pdp =

·qpd
(25)
where ARdp is the outlet-to-inlet area ratio of the sudden expan-
sion and qpd is the prediffuser exit dynamic pressure.
Computation of the dump area ratio ARdp has proven to be
somewhat problematic. The ﬂow in the dump region is highly
separated and an effective ﬂow area is difﬁcult to deﬁne. It is
therefore assumed in the present design that the ﬂow reattaches
in the annulus. Thus, the dump diffuser area ratio is given by:
ARdp = Aan
Apd
(26)
Premixer Design.
The premixer is designed to evapo-
rate the liquid fuel and mix the fuel vapour with air. Figure 5
illustrates the premixer concept and indicates the relevant param-
eters.
Precautions must be taken to ensure that the fuel/air mixture
does not react prior to entering the primary zone of the combus-
tor. Any potential ﬂameholders such as struts, fuel lines, hubs, or
any other bluff bodies where combustion can occur in its wake
must be eliminated. Therefore, the co-axial swirlers are designed
to ensure that no recirculation occurs inside the premixer [14].
Slow moving boundary layers on the premix tube walls must
also be minimized to ensure that the ﬂame will not creep up-
stream and cause ﬂashback.
The premixer tube converges to
continually accelerate the ﬂow and ﬂatten any thick, developed
boundary layers.
Chemical reaction can take place inside the premixer by au-
toignition after exposure to heated air. This spontaneous ignition
can be avoided

Lilley [17] states that strong swirl occurs when Sn > 0.6, which
is characterized by ﬂow recirculation.
Premixer Tube.
Ideally, the premixer should be de-
signed to ensure that complete evaporation and mixing occurs at
all operating conditions without the occurrence of autoignition.
However, this design considers only the higher power settings.
As a result of this decision, unevaporated droplets are expected
to escape the premixer at low power settings. These droplets pro-
duce small individual pilot ﬂames inside the liner and improve
the stable operating range of the combustor.
For simplicity, it is assumed that mixing occurs instanta-
neously and the length is based solely on considerations for evap-
oration. This implies that evaporation occurs at a much slower
rate than mixing, which is a reasonable assumption when the
temperature and pressure inside the premixer is low.
The evaporation time for the liquid fuel is estimated by
Figure 7.
THE DOME REGION.
modelling the spray as a single droplet with a diameter equal
to the Sauter mean diameter (SMD) of the spray. A relatively
simple Newtonian model was developed according to equations
in [4, 18, 19] for single-component vaporization with rapid in-
terna

title: Proceedings of GT2006
text:
Figure 8.
COMBUSTOR REACTOR MODEL.
den expansion losses incurred as the ﬂow enters the plenum and
the turning losses incurred as the ﬂow changes direction upon
entering the premixer.
Combustor Length.
The liner overall length is the sum
of the lengths of the primary and dilution zones, illustrated in
Figure 2.
The dilution zone provides the time for the hot primary zone
combustion gases to mix with the cooler annulus air. The length
of this zone is determined based on jet mixing considerations
since the combustor must provide the downstream turbine with a
speciﬁc pattern factor Q. The pattern factor is deﬁned as:
Q = Tmax −T4
T4 −T3
(37)
where Tmax is the maximum recorded temperature at the combus-
tor exit. The pattern factor is estimated by modelling the dilution
of the hot combustion gases with the colder jets of air admitted
through the dilution holes. This is discussed later.
Unlike the dilution zone, the length of the primary zone is
set to provide sufﬁcient combustion volume to operate stably and
efﬁciently. This value must typically be compromised to avoid an
excessively large combustor with a low volumetric heat release
rate.
The primary zone length is determined using reactor theory.
Reactor theory allows the designer to describe the detailed evolu-
tion of the reaction within the combustor using simpliﬁed chem-
ical reactors. The reader is directed to [7] for a mathematical
description of the different types of reactors.
The combustor is modelled using a simpliﬁed network con-
sisting of two reactors: a well-stirred reactor (WSR) and a plug-
ﬂow reactor (PFR) (Fig. 8). The WSR simulates combustion in
the primary zone while the PFR simulates the dilution of the
combustion products in the dilution zone. A 30-step reaction
mechanism devised by Kollrack [22] for modelling the combus-
tion of ”jet fuel”, represented as C12H23, was used.
Dilution Hole Design.
The liner dilution holes are de-
signed to provide the desired penetration of the admitted jets.
The ho

title: Proceedings of GT2006
text:
for round sharp-edged holes is provided by Kaddah [23]:
CD =
1.25(K −1)
[4K2 −K(2−β)2]0.5
(46)
where β is hole-to-annulus mass ﬂow rate ratio. The coefﬁcient
K is approximated by the following correlation [23]:
K = 1+0.64


q


(47)
where α is the hole-to-annulus cross-sectional area ratio and µ =
β/α.
Modelling the cool jet as it penetrates and mixes with the
hot combustion gases is useful for veriﬁcation of the hole design
procedure and estimation of the combustor exit pattern factor.
One empirical model for characterizing crossﬂowing jets is de-
scribed by Holdeman et al. [24]. The model provides a detailed
prediction of the jet penetration proﬁle.
Liner Wall Temperature.
Successful mechanical de-
sign requires adequate removal of heat to avoid damage of the
liner. The liner contains hot gases within it that are expected to
greatly exceed material limits, roughly 1100 K for nickel- and
cobalt-based alloys [9].
Designing a cooling scheme to ensure adequate heat removal
is facilitated by applying heat transfer theory. This allows one to
predict the effects of important design parameters on the liner
wall temperature. First, the wall temperature is computed ex-
cluding the effects of any supplemental cooling so that the de-
signer may gauge the cooling requirements. A cooling scheme is
designed and the liner is analyzed once again, this time including
the effects of supplemental wall cooling. In this case, the cool-
ing scheme uses ABC cooling by means of transverse turbulence
promoting ribs and a TBC.
The steady state uncooled wall temperature is computed us-
ing the simple heat transfer model developed by Lefebvre and
Herbert [25]. The heat transfer model uses empirical correlations
to estimate the various heat ﬂuxes and applies an energy balance
to solve for the metal temperature of the liner. The heat transfer
processes included are: conduction, internal and external radia-
tion, internal and external convection. The model was updated
ba

title: Proceedings of GT2006
text:
Combustor CFD.
Analysis of the reacting ﬂowﬁeld in-
side the liner was performed using CFX-5, which is a commer-
cially available CFD software package. The combustor ﬂow do-
main was modelled using a 1/10th section consisting of 150,000
nodes and 800,000 elements. This domain was solved with the
k-ε turbulence model, the combined Eddy Dissipation / Finite
Rate Chemistry model, and the Differential Approximation (P1)
radiation model.
Finite rate chemistry was approximated by
the two-step reaction mechanism for decane of Westbrook and
Dryer [28]. A two-step Zeldovich mechanism was added to com-
pute the mass fractions of thermal NO formed.
The velocity ﬁeld inside the combustor, predicted using
CFD, is depicted in Fig. 11.
A large torroidal recirculation
zone at the centre of the combustor is visible. This is typical
of strongly swirling ﬂows and indicates that the premixer blade
angle is appropriate. Additionally, adequate penetration of the
dilution jets can be observed. This indicates that the hole design
procedures functioned properly.
Figure 11.
PREDICTED COMBUSTOR VELOCITY FIELD (m/s).
Figure 12 illustrates the temperature contours along a cross-
section of the combustor, which were predicted using CFD. A
comparison between these values and those computed by the
preliminary design procedures is provided in Table 1.
While
good agreement between the predicted turbine inlet temperatures
(TIT) was observed, CFD computed a slightly higher ﬂame tem-
perature. These discrepancies are attributed to differences in re-
action mechanisms and model resolution.
Figure 12.
COMBUSTOR TEMPERATURE DISTRIBUTION (K).

dures and CFD are compared in Table 2. Small discrepancies be-
tween the two sets of values are observed that are primarily due to
differences in reaction mechanisms (i.e. 30-step versus 2-step

Premixer CFD.
Similar to the combustor, the ﬂowﬁeld
inside the premixer was solved with CFX-5. The premixer was
modelled using a 1/4 section and the domain was discretized us-
ing 125,000 nodes and 690,000 elements. No swirlers were in-
corporated in the simulation. The evaporating fuel spray issuing
from each nozzle was modelled as a single droplet and tracked
using a fully coupled evaporation model. While the effects of
turbulent dispersion were neglected, the analysis included the ef-
fects of drag and heat transfer on particle behaviour. The prop-
erties of diesel fuel were approximated using the predeﬁned ”Jet
A” model and the k-ε turbulence model was implemented.
Figure 14 illustrates the velocity ﬁeld inside the premixer
predicted using CFD. A small undesirable recirculation bubble
is observed along the premixer axis. Inside this potential ﬂame-
holder, however, the air/fuel ratio is too high for autoignition to
occur.
Figure 14.
PREDICTED PREMIXER VELOCITY FIELD (m/s).
The contours of fuel vapour mixedness along the outlet of
the premixer, which were predicted using CFD, are provided
in Figure 15. Mixedness is deﬁned as the local average mole
fraction divided by the maximum inlet mole fraction. Although
the droplets completely evaporate inside the premixer, multi-
ple pockets containing higher concentrations of fuel vapour are
observed. The inclusion of the effects of droplet size distribu-
tions, more realistic of actual sprays, is expected to yield more
favourable results.
Table 4 provides a comparison of the evaporation distance
predicted using CFD and the preliminary design procedures. A
large discrepancy was observed between the predicted values.
This was largely attributed to the different methods used for com-
puting the thermodynamic and transport properties of the vapour
phase surrounding the droplet. As these properties strongly af-
fect the evaporation
